---
title: "About Me"
toc: true
---

Hey there! 👋 I'm Laura. 

I have a background in both **molecular and systems neuroscience** and my work has spanned gut nervous system development to decision making in mice to mapping out the fruit fly's nervous system down.

When I'm not at my computer you can find me with my nose in a book (both fiction and non-fiction), listening to a podcast, running along trails, trying to grow enough basil to make "fresh" pesto year round, painting, drawing or making linoleum relief prints. I have enjoyed taking advantage of the international nature of a career in science and have lived in the UK, US, Austria and Australia. I'm only just noticing how 'Noah's Ark' that collection of countries is... 

                                            *

Over the course of my research career, I've realised that what I enjoy most is working with **data** — figuring out how to collect it well, present it clearly, and package it so that someone else can actually pick it up and use it. I care a lot about making research outputs genuinely reusable, not just technically available. I like thinking about possible non-traditional ways in which scientific knowledge might be shared and used based on the type of data involved and the people that might wish to access and use it in the future.

I've been fortunate to have worked in many different "types" of lab across the full range of the lab life cycle; from being a founding member of a new lab to joining a lab just before the lab head retires. I've learnt so much about how labs and the process of science work over this time and I spend a lot of time thinking about how to optimise lab workflows especially after discovering data management best practices (e.g. **FAIR** - Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable). 

I've worked on independent projects and in large-scale collaborations of >50 people and through leaving and joining multiple labs I have seen and been frustrated by the **leakage of knowledge** over time through poor data management, insufficient documentation and a lack of handover resources. The actions of past me are included in this frustration - case in point, I didn't start using GitHub for version control until the very end of my PhD... something that now haunts me greatly and is why I think I am now somewhat of a GitHub evangelist. 

It's not that I blame individuals. I think there's a real lack of guidance for research scientists and lab heads are often so busy managing people and trying to keep the lab afloat in the present moment that they don't have a lot of time and energy to think about future-proofing their knowledge. That's part of the reason why I wanted to include the [blog](/blog/index.qmd) page of this website to try and get down some of what I have learnt over the past decade or so. Hopefully it saves people from making the same mistakes as past-me - or at least saves them some of the pain later!
